items.q: In Milgram's study, what percentage of participants gave the maximum 450V shock?
items.why: Students remember 'most' obeyed and assume all did. Actually 65% went to 450V; 35% defied. This nuance matters because it shows obedience is strong but not absolute.

items.q: What did Bowlby mean by monotropy?
items.correct: One primary attachment figure (usually the mother) is qualitatively different and most important.
items.misconception: A child can only ever form one attachment.
items.why: Students take 'mono' literally as 'only one'. Bowlby recognised multiple attachments but argued the primary one was uniquely important for the internal working model.

items.q: When is deception in research ever acceptable?
items.correct: When demand characteristics would otherwise invalidate the study, with thorough debrief and ethics approval.
items.why: Students think BPS guidelines forbid deception outright. They actually permit it when justified, minimised, and followed by full debrief allowing withdrawal of data.

items.q: Top-down offender profiling was developed by:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: FBI. “Canter” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Differential association theory was proposed by:
items.why: It's Sutherland. “Eysenck” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Lombroso publish his atavistic form theory?
items.why: Exact answer: 1876 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
